summary: Spolsky discusses how small UI design decisions in community software profoundly shape the character and quality of online discussions. He explains why his own forum software deliberately omits threading and automatic quoting, drawing on observations from CompuServe, Usenet, and Echo. He argues that threading encourages gratuitous nitpicking and tangential replies, while a flat conversation structure forces discussions along a single coherent train of thought. The post also briefly mentions TechInterview moving to CityDesk.
key_points: Tiny changes in community software UI create dramatic differences in community behavior and culture
Threading encourages fragmented, nitpicky conversations where people branch off into trivial tangents
Removing automatic quoting leverages user laziness to filter out low-value responses
Flat (non-threaded) discussion forces conversation along a single coherent train of thought, improving readability
Moderation systems like Slashdot's threshold model solve the quality problem but create a choppy reading experience
money_quotes: I've long believed that tiny changes in the user interface of any kind of community software result in dramatic changes in the character of the community that you create.
Usenet resulted in the ultimate pathology: people quoting an entire three page article, and inserting their tedious nitpicks between every two lines.
Something interesting happens sociologically when you don't have threading: the conversation is forced along one train of thought.
If they want to nitpick about the third post and there are already twenty more posts after that, it's just too late.
Sheer laziness will prevent most people from tagging on comments that aren't an interesting extension to the current thread.
If you really want to set off on a tangent, you have to write something like 'I'd like to go back to what X said earlier…' which actually makes the whole topic read more coherently.
key_insight: The structure of community software — threading, quoting, reply mechanisms — shapes conversation quality more than moderation or rules ever can.
